focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 18

At dawn Halima took one handle of the trunk and Nura the other. Outside, the houses on Kadam Char already stood open-backed. Doors leaned in tied bundles against lane walls, bedframes came out through holes where woven partitions had been cut loose, and the air held the hot metal smell of tin sheets recently pried from nails. Nothing looked abandoned yet. It looked worked on, stripped for parts, a place people were still trying to win from the river one cooking pot and one hinge at a time.

Halima's own house had lost its modesty overnight. Once the bedding came off the walls and the rice bin was emptied and the low shelf cleared of oil bottles, the room showed every stain the years had put there: lamp smoke above the niche, old birth blood scrubbed pale into one corner of the floor, the dark thumb-worn patch beside the doorway where she had steadied herself so often that the mud plaster had turned smooth as fired clay. She stood a moment with her broad palm over that place. The wall still held the night's cool. By noon it would be somebody else's rubble or the river's.

Nura was already at work, fast and mean with it because that was how she handled anything that could still hurt her. She had rolled the mosquito net tight enough to choke it, tied the bedding with sari cloth, wrapped her school certificates and the nursing forms inside two layers of plastic, then pushed the packet high into the tin trunk beneath Halima's old shawls. The small burn mark on her wrist flashed whenever she reached across the room.

"Don't put the scissors at the bottom," she said without looking up. "If they search the bags again, they'll make a drama of it."

Halima dropped the cord-cutting scissors onto a folded blouse anyway. "Let them think an old dai can't travel without...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...o I tell them the father's name is still drying?"

Halima set Rahima's soap and the damp burial cloth on the threshold. "You stand with me. If they sort us late, you sleep where I sleep. If that office wants more paper, it can ask with my face in front of it."

"Your face didn't get us onto the clean list."

"No."

"Then don't talk like you've got a roof folded up somewhere for me."

Halima took that and did not step away from it. "I haven't. I've got a disputed note, Aminul's promise, and legs that still walk. That's what I've got."

Nura looked down at the packet in her hands. "I'm tired of futures that arrive as instructions."

"So am I," Halima said. "Wrap what stays dry. We'll fight the rest standing up."

From the western side of the char came a hammering line of sound where somebody else was taking down a roof. From Jamila's yard came the thin bright strike of Majed's bolts knocking together. From the path beyond the school came one raw burst of male grief that Kashem must have thought was quiet because he had tried to keep it inside his mouth.

Then a family from the eastern lane went past Halima's gate carrying their own front door on their shoulders, latch still swinging, the chalked claim number bright across the wood. Nura unwrapped the bundle she had just made and pulled the clean school certificates free from the smudged appeal slip. She tied the certificates back into cloth and slid them under the patched quilt. The appeal note she left by itself beneath an upside-down brass bowl so the wet ink would not touch anything else.

Halima stood under her own roof and did not call for a ladder. By noon other households' tin was already walking toward the ghat on men's backs. In her yard the roof stayed bolted overhead, and the appeal slip dried alone on the mat.
